coredata.dc:title: Application of pH-responsive functionalized hollow mesoporous organosilica nanoparticles for wood preservation

coredata.dc:description: Plant-derived preservatives exhibit excellent antifungal effects. However, their antifungal active ingredients can easily leach out. A properly designed functional response system can help realize the responsive release of preservatives and reduce leaching. Herein, the pH-responsive preservatives delivery system was designed and synthesized. The preservative (Forsythia) was encapsulated with hollow mesoporous organosilica nanoparticles (HMONs), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were employed as mesoporous plugging agents. Forsythia’s release was achieved by grafting pH-sensitive chemical bonds on the surface of the carrier. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and antifungal results showed that the pH-responsive switch was successfully broken. After 12 weeks of soil-jar incubation, the weight loss rate of treated wood was 2 % ± 0.5 %. The leaching of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs was lower than that of Forsythia under the same concentration. The design of the system reduced the leaching of Forsythia and extended the service life of wood. In addition, the system was also applicable to ammoniacal copper quat (ACQ). Forsythia–HMON–CQDs showed an outstanding fixation effect and antifungal ability, and its fluorescence can be used to track distribution in wood. This system can be extensively employed in protecting antifungal active ingredients and demonstrates an effective strategy for developing potential bio-based wood preservatives.

head.ce:title: Application of pH-responsive functionalized hollow mesoporous organosilica nanoparticles for wood preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plant-derived preservatives exhibit excellent antifungal effects. However, their antifungal active ingredients can easily leach out. A properly designed functional response system can help realize the responsive release of preservatives and reduce leaching. Herein, the pH-responsive preservatives delivery system was designed and synthesized. The preservative (Forsythia) was encapsulated with hollow mesoporous organosilica nanoparticles (HMONs), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were employed as mesoporous plugging agents. Forsythia’s release was achieved by grafting pH-sensitive chemical bonds on the surface of the carrier. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and antifungal results showed that the pH-responsive switch was successfully broken. After 12 weeks of soil-jar incubation, the weight loss rate of treated wood was 2 % ± 0.5 %. The leaching of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs was lower than that of Forsythia under the same concentration. The design of the system reduced the leaching of Forsythia and extended the service life of wood. In addition, the system was also applicable to ammoniacal copper quat (ACQ). Forsythia–HMON–CQDs showed an outstanding fixation effect and antifungal ability, and its fluorescence can be used to track distribution in wood. This system can be extensively employed in protecting antifungal active ingredients and demonstrates an effective strategy for developing potential bio-based wood preservatives.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data used to support the findings of this study are included within the article and the supplementary information file.

ce:section.ce:para: Wood degradation is a considerable challenge faced by the construction, shipbuilding, and furniture industries [1,2]. Owing to the degradation of wood rotting fungi, mechanical properties of wood are reduced, affording massive monetary losses [3]. Traditional chemical preservatives (chromate copper arsenate or pentachlorophenol) negatively impact the ecological environment [4,5]. Thus, the eco-friendly treatment method is promising [6].

Previous studies have shown that many natural products such as tannins and peel extract can be extracted from plants for use in the protection of wood-based materials [7,8]. Compared with chemical preservatives, natural preservatives extracted from plants exhibit excellent antifungal effects. Further, the physical and mechanical properties of treated wood are not reduced by the use of natural preservatives [9]. However, recent studies have found that the antifungal active ingredients of natural preservatives can easily be leached when exposed to water, causing wastage of preservatives and a shortened service life of preservatives, affording considerable monetary losses [10]. As a plant extract used for wood protection, Forsythia has also several disadvantages such as easy wastage, short efficacy period, and easy leaching [11]. Therefore, a novel system to enhance the leaching resistance, reduce wastage, and prolong the efficacy period of Forsythia is urgently required.

In the methods used for preventing fungi, bacteria, and treating cancer, nanodrug delivery systems have attracted increasing attention owing to their high efficiency, targeting, and safety [12]. Many studies have shown that developing a drug delivery system mainly involves the selection of a carrier, establishment of a plugging agent, design of a response mode, and preparation of antibacterial drugs [13,14]. Among many drug carriers, hollow mesoporous organosilica nanoparticle (HMON) has the advantages of large cavity, high drug loading, and easily functionalized surface modification [15]. However, owing to the open pores of HMON, the release rate of antibacterial agents encapsulated by HMON is rapid, considerably limiting the application of HMON as a carrier of antibacterial drug delivery [16]. To realize a slow release of the agent, it is important to select the appropriate plugging agent to plug the pores of HMON. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a zero-dimensional carbon material exhibiting low toxicity, small particle size, abundant surface groups, distinctive fluorescence properties, and low cost [17]. CQDs are extensively employed in the fields of biomarkers and fluorescence tracking owing to their distinctive fluorescence properties, and their small particle size and abundant surface functional groups make it possible for them to be used as pore-plugging agents [18]. During the synthesis of a drug delivery system, controlling the drug in HMON to achieve timing positioning (accurate) release is a challenging problem. Environmental responsiveness refers to the dynamic effect on the interface of materials through environmental changes (temperature, pH, enzymes, etc.) [19]. The design of functional responsiveness can improve the utilization rate of drugs and reduce waste and pollution [20]. Therefore, the design of an environmentally responsive drug delivery system can effectively resolve the disadvantages of Forsythia.

Herein, we propose an ingenious method to realize the pH-response release of Forsythia and reduce leaching. The pH changes considerably during the initial decay stage of Pinus sylvestris. Thus, HMON and CQDs were used as the carrier and plugging agents of Forsythia, respectively, to design and synthesize a pH-responsive preservatives delivery system. The response-release ability of the system under different pH conditions and the antibacterial effect in vitro were studied, and the changes in the system before and after the grafting of CQDs were analyzed. The system was exposed to water to evaluate its leaching resistance. After being impregnated into the wood, the system was investigated for its distribution in wood and its resistance to fungal growth. The results show that the system exhibits a good response-release ability, antifungal and antileaching effects, and excellent fluorescence characteristics. The design strategy of pH-response system is promising in many fields, such as wood protection and preservative delivery.

Equipment manufactured by Thermo Scientific (Suzhou, China) provided ultrapure water (resistivity > 18.2 MΩ cm). Merck (Nantong, Jiangsu province, China) supplied sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl). According to the actual demand (Hohhot, Inner Mongolia, China), Sinopharm Holdings Co. ltd. provided tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) solution (25 wt% in H2O), bis[3-(triethoxysilyl) propyl] tetrasulfide (BTES), ammonia solution (25 %–28 %), 3-aminopropyltriethoxy-silane (APTES), NaCl, Na2CO3, citric acid, triethanolamine (TEA), carbamide (CO(NH2)2), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHs), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylamino propyl) carbodiimide (EDC), methanol (CH3OH), ethanol (C2H5OH), glucose (C6H12O6), and agar (C12H18O9)n. The purity of experimental drugs is analytical-grade and they can be used without purification.

Wood blocks (20 × 10 × 20 mm3) (tangential × longitudinal × radial) were produced from the sapwood of Pinus sylvestris (Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica Litv.) lumber exhibiting an air-dried density of 0.48 g/cm−3. The wood specimens (2–4 growth rings/cm) were obtained from freshly felled trees and dried at 60 °C. Wood blocks were not degraded and were untreated with preservatives, and there were no visible knots or deposits of resins. Forsythia (Forsythia suspensa (Thunb.) Vahl) fruit was obtained from a national local pharmacy (Inner Mongolia, China). The microbiology institute of China (Beijing, China) provided the strain of G. trabeum (Gloeophyllum trabeum (Pers.: Fr.) Murr.).

An atomic fluorescence spectrophotometer (AFS9700, Beijing, China) was used to measure the fluorescence properties of the Forsythia–HMON–CQDs preservative. The surface morphologies and distribution of CQDs and HMON were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEOL, JEM-2100, Japan). The working distance was set to 3 mm, and the acceleration voltage was 5 kV. HMON spectrum and elemental analyses were conducted using energy dispersive spectroscopy(EDS). The structures and particle size of CQDs and HMON were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Tecnai F30, Netherlands). The sample was placed on a copper grid (400 mesh) and the TEM acceleration voltage was set as 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to determine the infrared absorption peak and group stretching vibration of the sample after KBr mixed pressing (Nicolet is 10, United States). The 4 cm−1 resolution was set between 400 and 4000 cm−1. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Dynapro Titan TC, United States) was used to measure the size of HMON in ultrapure water, and the index of refraction was set to 1.33. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to examine the chemical properties and related compositions of CQDs and HMON by measuring the binding energy of electrons (ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher Scientific, United States). The vacuum of the analysis room was 8 × 10−10 Pa, and the passing energy was 69 ev. The chemical structure and molecular interactions of HMON were obtained using Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw inVa, Britain). The distribution of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs in wood was obtained using confocal laser scanning microscopy (Zeiss LSM880NLO, Germany). Each sample was tested thrice for accurate data.

The HMON was prepared by enhancing the selective etching route based on the typical Stöber approach in this experiment [21]. First, 74 mL ethanol, 3.14 mL ammonia, and 10 mL ultrapure water were thoroughly mixed in a water bath (30 °C) for 5 min, and 6 mL TEOS liquid was added to the above solution. Subsequently, the mixed solution system was stirred for 1 h using a stirrer, centrifuged to obtain SiO2 nanoparticles, washed twice with ethanol, and dispersed in 60 mL ultrapure water. Further, CTAC was employed as an organic template for HMON, and mesoporous silicone was coated on SiO2 nanoparticles. For 1.5 h, 2.00 g CTAC and 0.04 g TEA were vigorously stirred in 90 mL ultrapure water. Afterward, >30 mL of SiO2 solution was blended and stirred with a stirrer for 1.5 h. Thereafter, 1.8 mL of BTES was added dropwise, stirred at 80 °C for 1 h, centrifuged to obtain SiO2@MON particles, washed twice with ethanol, and dispersed in 30 mL ultrapure water. Na2CO3 was employed as an etchant to selectively remove the internal SiO2 core and a cavity was prepared. The above SiO2@MON was dispersed into 0.6 M Na2CO3 aqueous solution and stirred for 1 h at 80 °C. After centrifugation, the HMON particles were obtained by washing twice with ultrapure water. Finally, the resulting HMON particles were extracted using NaCl in 30 mL of methanol (1 wt%) for 6 h to remove CTAC. After several extractions, the final HMON product was dispersed in 30 mL ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Forsythia solution and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, Forsythia was chosen as the guest molecule to serve as an antifungal preservative. Forsythia was powdered and sieved (80 mesh), and the powder was dried at 80 °C in an oven. Forsythia powder (72 g) and ethanol (100 mL) were added to prepare Forsythia solutions of different concentrations. The power of the microwave reaction was 300 W, microwave time was 2 min, and the extraction was repeated thrice.

The mesoporous plugging agents (CQDs) were prepared using citric acid and urea. In the reaction of citric acid and urea, the bottom-up synthesis approach was selected, and 2 g of solid citric acid was first added to 10 mL ultrapure water as a carbon source, followed by 1 g of urea as a precursor material for the synthesis of CQDs. The abovementioned solution was heated at high temperature for 5 min to obtain a brown solid substance. The obtained solid CQDs were dispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water using ultrasound. The solution was centrifuged at 1792 RCF for 4 min to extract the supernatant, which was treated with a dialysis bag for 48 h to remove excess precursors. The dialysis medium was ultrapure water. The maximum absorbance solution of the preliminarily obtained CQDs was adjusted to 0.5 and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of pH in decayed wood
ce:section.ce:para: According to the Chinese standard GB/T 6043–2009 (2009b) [23], the change in pH value during the decay of Pinus sylvestris was investigated. First, 240 g potatoes were diced and added to 1250 mL of distilled water. Then, the mixture was boiled and filtered through gauze to retain the potato supernatant. Agar (24 g) and glucose (33 g) were added to the supernatant. For 40 min, the solution was sterilized at a high temperature (121 °C) and pressure (0.08 Mpa). The above culture medium was poured into a petri dish and allowed to cool, and then G. trabeum was inoculated and cultivated for 7 d at 27 °C and 80 % humidity. The sterilized wood samples were placed in petri dishes filled with G. trabeum. The petri dish was placed in an incubator with a temperature of 27 °C and 80 % humidity. Finally, the incubation period was set at 4 weeks, and the first, second, third, and fourth weeks of the wood blocks were selected for pH value measurement. This was done to investigate the pH value change during the decay process to determine the optimal pH-response point and to design responsive chemical bonds.

The approach to measuring the pH value of the decayed samples was to remove the decayed test pieces from the culture bottle, and mycelium was scraped off the specimen surface. The decayed test pieces were cut into small pieces and crushed in a high-speed grinder, and a standard sieve (40 mesh) was employed to sieve the wood powder. 3 g of wood powder was put in a 50 mL glass beaker and mixed with 30 mL of distilled water that had been boiled and cooled. After stirring the wood powder and water solution for 5 min and letting it stand for 15 min, the pH meter was employed to measure the solution pH, and this step was repeated thrice. The five groups of samples were measured every week; the average value of the five groups was used as the final pH result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs and responsiveness testing
ce:section.ce:para: The purpose of HMON reaction with the silane coupling agent APTES is to graft amino groups to form amino-functional HMON. To obtain amino-functionalized HMON, HMON (20 mg) was added to 6 mL ultrapure water; EDC (38 mg) and NHS (57 mg) were mixed in the above solution, and APTES (45 µL) was added to the system and stirred using a stirrer for 8 h. The HMON particles were purified by centrifugation and the product was washed thrice with ultrapure water. Finally, the modified HMON was placed in an oven to dry to a constant weight. In ultrapure water, the Forsythia solution was mixed with the amino-functionalized HMON. The solution reacted for 3 h at 75 °C in a hydrothermal reactor to prepare Forsythia–HMON. This was done to fill the HMON with Forsythia. To prepare Forsythia–HMON–CQDs, the Forsythia–HMON was dissolved in the CQDs (4 mL).

The internal environment of decaying wood was simulated by determining the optimal pH value during the decay process of Pinus sylvestris, and the buffer solution was prepared according to the optimal pH value. Forsythia–HMON–CQDs were put inside a dialysis bag (44 mm, MD40-100), and the dialysis bag was left for 12 h. The changes and release effects of chemical bonds before and after dialysis were investigated using dialysis technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MIC values for Forsythia and Forsythia–HMON–CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: MIC result was employed to demonstrate the antifungal effect in vitro [24]. The concentrations of Forsythia (8, 10, 20, 40, and 60 mg/mL), Forsythia–HMON–CQDs (10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 mg/mL), and the control group (without preservative) were set. Subsequently, the above treatment solution was added to the PDA medium (natural pH value); a new control group was added during this process, and Forsythia–HMON–CQDs were placed into the PDA medium (pH = 4.7) to analyze the response release. G. trabeum was inoculated into the medium when the PDA medium was solidified. After inoculation, the petri dish was placed in an incubator (28 °C and 75 % RH) for 7 d, and the mycelium diameter was recorded every day from the third day. For each concentration, five groups of parallel experiments were set; the MIC data were repeated five times and the average was recorded as the final data.

The American Standard (AWPA-E11) was used to examine the leaching effect of preservatives during exposure to water [25]. In a vacuum environment, the wood blocks were treated for 40 min with Forsythia, ACQ, and Forsythia–HMON–CQDs. Particularly, the wood blocks were air-dried and weighed. Six samples were chosen for each treatment group and repeated thrice. The preservative-treated sample was placed in a 500 mL beaker, and deionized water (180 mL) was added to immerse the sample. At intervals of 6, 24, and 48 h, the samples were stirred and quantitatively replaced with deionized water. Afterward, deionized water was replaced every 48 h. The leaching test lasted for 14 d, and the leaching solution was collected for content analysis.

Wood blocks (Pinus sylvestris) without discoloration, decay, or visible defects were selected for analysis. The samples were cleaned with a sandpaper to remove burrs. The wood blocks were dried to a constant weight (W1 ) at 103 °C for decay resistance testing. The decay performance of wood was tested based on the Chinese standard GB/T 13942.1–2009 [26]. First, river sand (75 g), Pinus sylvestris powder (8 g), maltose (60 mL), cornstarch (4 g), and brown sugar (0.5 g) were mixed in a culture flask as a medium for fungal growth. Three pieces of wood with dimensions of 20 mm × 5 mm × 20 mm (T × L × R) were placed on top of the medium to function as the feeding wood. The culture flask was sterilized at a high temperature (121 °C) for 30 min, and then G. trabeum was inoculated and cultured (28 °C and 75 % RH) for 7 d. Second, the control group (blank) and three treatment groups (Forsythia, Forsythia–HMON–CQDs, and ACQ–HMON–CQDs) with different concentrations (0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.12, and 0.14 g/mL) were used for testing. The decay resistance effects of the control and the treatment groups were repeated five times. The wood blocks were impregnated with various concentrations of preservatives under vacuum pressure infusion for 60 min. After impregnation, the liquid on the wood surface was cleaned and the weight (W2 ) of the wood was recorded. The wood blocks from the treatment groups were placed in a drying oven at 103 °C, and the weight was recorded after it reached a constant value (W3 ). Finally, wood blocks from the treatment and control groups were placed on the feeding wood when G. trabeum overgrew the surface of the feeding wood, and the culture flask was cultured for 12 weeks. Mycelium was removed from the wood surface, and the weight (W4 ) of the wood blocks was recorded after drying. The preservative loading of wood was calculated using Equation (1).
(1)
L =
W 2

W 1
× C × 10
V
where W1 represents the weight before preservative treatment (g), W2 represents the weight after preservative treatment (g), V represents the volume of the wood block (cm3), and C is the concentration of preservative solutions. The rate of wood weight loss was obtained owing to fungal exposure using Equation (2).
(2)
R =
W 3

W 4
W 3
where W3 represents the weight of preservative treatment after drying, ML represents the weight loss rate during decay of the wood sample, and W4 represents the constant weight of the decayed sample. Based on weight loss rates, wood decay resistance grades are divided into four categories. First class (0 %–10 %) is the high decay resistance, second class (11 %–24 %) is the decay resistance, third class (25 %–44 %) is the slight decay resistance, and fourth class (˃45 %) is no decay resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design conception and responsiveness of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Plant-derived wood preservatives are ecofriendly; however, the characteristics of easy leaching of the natural active ingredients limit their further applications. The development of an intelligent sensitive preservative carrier system can realize a controlled release of plant extracts and reduce the leaching rate. The main components of wood are cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. In the early stage of wood (Pinus sylvestris) degradation, wood rotting fungi secrete a large amount of cellulase, oxalic acid, and other acidic substances, which gradually decrease the pH value inside wood [27]. The change in pH value has laid a foundation for the design of a pH-responsive preservative delivery system. Scheme 1 shows the design method of the pH-response system and the flowchart used in the field of wood protection. Herein, Forsythia was used as a preservative, the response switch was designed for pH change, and the amide bond was selected owing to its pH sensitivity. An amide bond is formed between amino and carboxyl groups. Based on this, the surface of HMON was functionalized with amino groups, and the surface of CQDs had carboxyl groups that can be used as mesoporous plugging agents. The amino-functionalized HMON and CQDs will further combine to form pH-sensitive amide bonds. When the pH of wood reaches the response point, the amide bond is broken, and the Forsythia encapsulated by HMON is released. Leaching tests indicated that the leaching rate of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs during exposure to water was reduced. XPS, MIC, and decay resistance tests confirmed the response release of Forsythia and proved the feasibility of the design concept.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Change in pH value and establishment of response mode
ce:section.ce:para: Table S1 illustrates the pH change of Pinus sylvestris wood (without preservatives) at various decay times. The pH value of the wood specimens without decay was 5.25, and the pH of the decayed specimens for 1 week was 4.22. With increasing decay time, the pH value of the decayed samples decreased continuously owing to the degradation products of G. trabeum. The products (organic acids, oxalic acid, and sugar acids) formed during the degradation of wood cell walls are acidic, which may decrease the pH value [27,28]. The previous studies conducted a detailed analysis of acid substances, which are related to the hydrolysis products of cellulase during wood degradation [29]. The rate of pH change (4.22–5.25) was the most noticeable during the first week of early decay ( Fig. S1 ). This is an interesting result because a change in the pH value makes it possible to design a pH-responsive preservative release system. Selecting an appropriate pH-sensitive bond is important for pH response. Amide bonds are prone to cleavage at a pH of approximately 4.7 [30]; thus, they were employed as pH-responsive chemical bonds. The amide bond is formed by the condensation of carboxyl and amino groups, and it is necessary to functionalize the surface amino groups of HMON. CQDs with carboxyl groups were condensed with amino groups of HMON to form amide bonds as the pH-responsive switch. The amide bonds were broken because of the pH response, and the preservative was released when the wood was degraded by G. trabeum [31].

SEM results demonstrated that HMON was uniformly distributed and had well-dispersed spheres without visible overlapping stickiness, and the average particle size was ∼ 200 nm (Fig. 1 a, b). The good dispersion of HMON carrier was conducive to the uniform distribution of Forsythia in the wood cell wall rather than to the tight stacking on the surface of the wood. In addition, agglomerated nanocarriers pose a series of problems, such as uneven surface roughness and waste of reagents [35,36]. The excellent dispersion of the HMON carrier provided a basis for the pH-responsive release of Forsythia. The inset in Fig. 1b shows the mesopores of average size (2–2.5 nm); the analysis of mesopore size can help select the appropriate pore-plugging agent. The TEM results also showed that the SiO2 nanoparticles were spherical and uniformly dispersed (Fig. 1 c).The core–shell structure of nanocarriers is represented in Fig. 1d, which shows that MON shells were successfully coated on the SiO2 surface, and the mesopores on the surface can be seen (Fig. 1 d). The inner SiO2 core was removed by Na2CO3 solution, and the hollow structure was visible in the TEM results of HMON (Fig. 1 e). The concentration of Na2CO3 has an important influence on the removal of the internal SiO2 core. TEM images showed that SiO2@MON treated with 0.6 M Na2CO3 exhibited a more complete hollow structure. Conversely, low concentration formed a small hollow structure, which was unfavorable to the loading of Forsythia ( Fig. S2 ). EDS results showed that the elements of HMON were O, N, Si, S, and C, their atomic percentage was 50.54 %, 5.87 %, 19.67 %, 3.33 %, and 20.59 %, respectively. The elements were all shown in the element map, demonstrating that HMON has successfully undergone disulfide hybridization (Fig. 1 f, S3) [37]. DLS results also confirmed that HMON particle size was ∼ 200 nm (Fig. 1 g). The particle size of the nanocarrier is the key to entering the wood interior. The 200-nm size shows that it is easier to enter the cell wall of wood, which can protect the wood from degradation to a greater extent [38]. The Raman spectrum of HMON showed the stretching vibration of the –SS– bond at 520 cm−1, confirming the combination of TEOS and BTES in the framework (Fig. 1 h). This was consistent with EDS results.

To directly reflect the synthesis process, the synthesis model of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs is illustrated (Fig. 2
a). HMON showed visible hollow structure features, and the inner solid SiO2 core was completely etched to form a cavity with a diameter of around 200 nm (Fig. 2 b). To form an amino-functionalized HMON, HMON reacted with APTES, and the Forsythia was impregnated into the HMON. Forsythia was successfully loaded and filled the internal cavity of HMON (Fig. 2 c). The successful loading of Forsythia indicated that HMON was feasible as a preservative carrier.

CQDs were effectively synthesized in one step using citric acid as the precursor carbon source and urea as the surface modifier. Citric acid contains numerous carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, causing carbonization and dehydration after microwave irradiation and facilitating the formation of carbon nuclei. TEM characterization showed the size and morphology of the uniformly dispersed CQDs, which were almost monodisperse spherical particles. The homogeneous dispersion of CQDs may be more conducive to the formation of amide bonds. The particle-size distribution diagram demonstrates that the CQDs had a narrow particle-size distribution, and the average particle size was 2.5 nm (Fig. 2 d). From the viewpoint of particle size, it is suitable to obtain CQDs using a microwave-assisted approach because it can completely block the mesopores on the surface of HMON. Urea was employed as a surface modifier to dope carbon dots with nitrogen elements, which enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. The emission spectra of CQDs under different wavelength excitations were investigated to further analyze the optical properties of CQDs, and the optimal excitation wavelength of CQDs was employed to determine the optimal fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence results showed that the prepared CQDs had dual emission peaks [39,40], with maximum emission wavelengths of 440 nm and 536 nm and excitation wavelengths (λex) of 360 nm and 450 nm, to achieve the maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2 e), indicating that the emission wavelength and intensity were closely related to the excitation wavelength.

To prevent the rapid release of Forsythia, the prepared CQDs were dissolved in the Forsythia–HMON solution. CQDs were employed as the surface mesoporous plugging agents in this process. Digital photos of Forsythia–HMON and Forsythia–HMON–CQDs in solutions before (up) and after (down) centrifugation are shown (Fig. 2 f). Compared with the white Forsythia–HMON, yellow Forsythia–HMON–CQDs showed that CQDs had been successfully combined with HMON. The clear supernatant after centrifugation confirmed the covalent binding of CQDs to the HMON surface and no significant leaks. The FTIR spectrum demonstrated that the characteristic peaks of Forsythia were 1500 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1. All of them appear on Forsythia–HMON–CQDs, confirming the successful loading of Forsythia into HMON. The broad characteristic appeared around 3435 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of the OH bond. The narrow peak at 1635 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of CC/CC and CO; the results showed that CQDs had carboxyl groups and good water solubility [41]. The characteristic peaks of HMON at 1093 cm−1 and 500 cm−1 confirmed the SiOSi and SS bonds in HMON. The characteristic peaks of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs at 3400 cm−1 showed that amide bonds were formed on the surface through the crosslinking process between carboxyl and amine ( Fig. S4 ). The Forsythia–HMON–CQDs were observed in the wood cell wall and were uniformly distributed, indicating that the system was permeable to the cell wall of wood (Pinus sylvestris) [42]. The presence of more Forsythia–HMON–CQDs in the wood pits and conduits showed that the system was impregnated into the wood through the pits on the conduits. In addition, appropriate temperature (28 °C) has a key impact on the growth of G. trabeum. When the temperature was higher than this, the growth of G. trabeum was gradually inhibited. Eventually, wood cannot be degraded [43]. The temperature was also an important factor affecting the stability of fluorescence [44]. Based on this, we analyzed the fluorescence stability of the Forsythia–HMON–CQDs system at different temperatures (5 °C, 20 °C, 35 °C, and 50 °C). Figure S5 showed the change in fluorescence intensity of the system after 2 h. Although temperature affects the fluorescence intensity, the system maintains good fluorescence stability. Notably, it usually does not require higher temperatures and prolonged observation for the impregnation of wood preservatives and growth of G. trabeum [43]. The outstanding fluorescence properties of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs can track their distribution in the wood ( Fig. S6 ).

The responsiveness of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs was primarily offered by amide bonds; thus, the responsiveness was examined by the synthesis and cleavage of amide bonds. In the above results, the optimal pH-response point was established (pH = 4.7), the HMON–CQDs were placed in the dialysis bag, and the buffer solution (pH = 4.7) was employed to simulate the internal environment of the early decayed wood. Dialysis and XPS were effective methods to analyze the changes of chemical bonds before and after dialysis (Fig. 3
a).

The fluorescence spectra of CQDs before and after dialysis demonstrated visible bimodal position changes (Fig. 2 e, 3b). Further, CQDs retained fluorescent properties after dialysis ( Fig. S7 ). The double peaks after dialysis showed that the CQDs exhibited a polychromatic effect, and chemical bonds were broken [45]. The formation of urea-modified CQDs can be confirmed from the XPS spectral test findings, and the surface elements and chemical states of CQDs were investigated (Fig. 3 c–h). Figure S8 shows the full XPS full spectrum of CQDs. The surface of the CQDs has only three elements, indicating that the CQDs were formed from the original carbon source with almost no impurities [46]. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum showed a very distinct peak at 284.6–288.2 eV, corresponding to the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms present on CQDs, confirming the CQDs framework formation and composition. The XPS spectrum of high-resolution C1s of CQDs was identified with three peaks by deconvolution at 284.9 eV, 287.2 eV, and 288.5 eV, which correspond to the CC/CC, CO, and OCO functional groups, respectively (Fig. 3 c). The results indicated that the CQDs exhibited a good lattice structure [47]. Fig. 3d shows the spectrum of O1s XPS. The deconvolution of the band was performed on the two peaks located at 529.8 eV and 530.8 eV, which belong to the COH and COOH groups, respectively, demonstrating the presence of surface carboxyl of CQDs (Fig. 3 d). Fig. 3 e shows the deconvolution XPS spectrum of N1s. The peaks appear at 397.5 eV and 398.1 eV, which belong to NO and CN/CN, respectively. This is related to trace amounts of nitrogen entering the carbon core of CQDs [48]. Fig. 3 f shows the deconvolution C1s spectrum of CQDs after dialysis. There are visible peaks at 285.2 eV and 286.8 eV, which correspond to CC/CC and CO, respectively. The disappearance of OCO showed that CQDs and HMON were effectively separated after dialysis. The O1s XPS spectra of the CQDs after dialysis indicated visible peak changes at 531.1 eV and 530.1 eV, which belonged to COH and COOH, respectively (Fig. 3 g). Fig. 3 h shows the deconvolution XPS spectrum of N1s after dialysis. The peaks appeared at 398.1 eV and 400.8 eV, which belong to CN/CN and NO, respectively. The shifts of the peaks showed that the chemical bonds and properties of the CQDs had changed after grafting and dialysis [49].

Fig. 4
a, e, i show deconvolution C1s spectra of HMON before and after grafting CQDs, and after treatment with dialysis technology. There are visible peaks in the spectrogram, which belong to CO, CO/CN, and CC. The shift of the C1s peak position showed that the valence state of C1s has changed or the coordination atoms have changed. It was established that chemical bonds are formed and broken during grafting and dialysis. The O1s spectra of HMON before grafting and after dialysis can be deconvoluted into a peak at 530.9 eV, corresponding to CO (Fig. 4 b, j). The deconvoluted O1s XPS spectrum of HMON was shown after grafting the CQDs (Fig. 4 f). There are visible peaks at 530.0 eV and 530.9 eV, which belong to CO and CO, respectively [50]. The CO appeared on the HMON surface after grafting the CQDs, indicating that CQDs were successfully grafted onto the HMON, and the amino groups on the surface of the HMON and the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs were successfully condensed into amide bonds. After dialysis, the CO of HMON disappeared, indicating that the amide bond between CQDs and HMON was broken. The high-resolution Si2p XPS deconvolution spectra of HMON before grafting, after grafting, and after dialysis represented the change in peaks, which belong to SiO and SiC (Fig. 4 c, g, k). The shift of the Si2p peak position after grafting showed that the CQDs were successfully grafted to the HMON surface [51]. The position of the peak changed again after dialysis, indicating that the CQDs were separated. During grafting and dialysis, the chemical bonds on the surface of HMON changed. The high-resolution S2p XPS spectra of HMON before grafting, after grafting, and after dialysis can be deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to SS and –SS– (Fig. 4 d, h, l). The peak of –SS– showed that the silicone shell on the HMON surface was successfully formed, which was also consistent with the results of Raman spectroscopy. The shift of the peak position demonstrated the successful combination of CQDs with the silica shell on the surface of HMON, and the results were consistent with the results of C1s, O1s, and Si2p. Figure S9 shows the N1s deconvolution spectrum formed by HMON-grafted CQDs. The visible peaks appear at 400.3 eV and 398.0 eV, which belong to CN/CN and NO, respectively [52]. This indicated the formation of an amide bond between HMON and CQDs. The XPS findings of HMON before grafting showed the successful bridging of disulfide bonds. The XPS results of HMON after grafting the CQDs indicated that the carboxyl of CQDs and amino groups of HMON were successfully dehydrated and condensed to form amide bonds. The XPS findings of HMON after dialysis demonstrated that the amide bond between HMON and CQDs was successfully broken, which was consistent with the XPS results of CQDs and FTIR results [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of responsiveness and antifungal effects in vitro

ce:section.ce:para: Table S2 shows the leaching effects of Forsythia, Forsythia–HMON–CQDs, and ACQ exposed to water. The leaching (L) gradually decreased with increasing preservative loading (Ret) for Forsythia and Forsythia–HMON–CQDs. The leaching of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs was lower than that of Forsythia under the same concentration, indicating that the preservative carrier could enhance Forsythia’s leaching resistance. To verify the leaching effect, the ACQ was encapsulated into a carrier system, and the results demonstrated that ACQ’s leaching resistance was enhanced. This demonstrates that the carrier system was universal. The leaching resistance was enhanced because Forsythia–HMON–CQDs were evenly attached to the cell wall surface of the treated wood ( Fig. S12 ), and the three-dimensional nanonetwork structure of silica further improved the fixation effect of Forsythia inside the wood [54].

The absorption and retention of Forsythia, Forsythia–HMON–CQDs, and ACQ–HMON–CQDs preservatives after impregnated wood are shown (Fig. 5
a-c). In this process, a large number of particles can be filled inside the wood under the condition of vacuum pressure owing to the small size of nanoparticles, which increases the Forsythia fixation and reduces the leaching. The decay resistance of wood demonstrated that the blank specimen was completely covered by G. trabeum. However, the Forsythia-loaded HMON demonstrated a good antifungal effect (Fig. 5 d), indicating that Forsythia was successfully released in the pH response. After 12 weeks of the decay test, the weight loss rate of the sample in the blank group was 48 %. Blank samples had no decay resistance, according to the definition of wood decay resistance grade. Although the effect of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs was not as noticeable as that of ACQ–HMON–CQDs, the treatment group with Forsythia–HMON–CQDs achieved a high decay resistance grade and exhibited good resistance to fungi growth (Fig. 5 e).

The findings revealed that Forsythia, as a wood preservative, had an obvious inhibiting effect on the growth of G. trabeum. Increasing the concentration of Forsythia on the antifungal effect should be investigated further in the future. As a type of plant-derived natural wood preservative, Forsythia has the advantage of environmental protection. However, it has the disadvantage of easy leaching. The construction of the Forsythia–HMON–CQDs system can realize the responsive release of Forsythia and reduce Forsythia leaching.

Based on the antifungal effect of environmentally friendly Forsythia, a preservative delivery system (Forsythia–HMON–CQDs) was designed to achieve the pH-responsive release of Forsythia and improve its antileaching performance. In this process, HMON showed satisfactory dispersion as a preservative carrier and achieved the encapsulation of Forsythia. The pH change in the early stage of wood decay was employed as the response point. Forsythia–HMON–CQDs exhibited the characteristic of pH response by constructing an amide bond. The response release of Forsythia and the plugging of mesopores by CQDs were confirmed through biological experiments. The wood treated with Forsythia–HMON–CQDs exhibits good resistance to fungi growth after 12 weeks of soil-jar incubation. Fluorescence of Forsythia–HMON–CQDs can be used to track their distribution in wood to achieve visual effects. The design of the system improved the antileaching ability and increased the efficacy period of Forsythia. In addition, the leaching resistance of ACQ was also improved. Therefore, the designed delivery system has broad application prospects in the fields of antibacterial agent delivery, response release, and wood protection.
